text: Mainstream accounts of Indonesian foreign policy have tended to suggest that Islam has played little part in shaping Jakarta’s international conduct. Yet an exploration of Indonesia’s diplomatic history during the country’s struggle for independence from Dutch colonial rule between 1945 and 1949 suggests otherwise. It is argued here that the decentralized nature of the struggle for Indonesian independence provided unprecedented opportunities for non-state and civil society organizations to engage in foreign policy activities. The official diplomatic history of Indonesia also confirms that the new nation made considerable use of the Islamic dimension of its international identity in order to cultivate good relations with the Arab world. Early international recognition of Indonesia was secured for the new republic with the support of Arab and Muslim countries, and Indonesia’s involvement in the Islamic Movement for Pakistan following India’s decision to oppose Pakistan’s membership of the United Nations also provided further support to the Indonesian government’s campaign for independence. However, as Indonesian diplomacy became more formally institutionalized and diplomatic relations with other Western and non-Communist countries developed more rapidly after 1948, the salience of Islam and of Indonesian–Arab relations in Indonesian foreign policy began to decline.
